title: Jeremiah 37
subtitle: Jeremiah Imprisoned

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Siege of Jerusalem

heb.paragraph: "Do not deceive yourselves (tashshi'u naphshotekem)" (v.9). The reflexive form emphasizes self-deception: "do not lift up your own souls" with false hope. When the Egyptian army approaches and the Babylonians temporarily withdraw, the people assume the siege is over. Jeremiah warns: the Babylonians will return and burn the city.

refs.note: Isaiah warned against Egyptian alliances a century earlier: "Woe to those who go down to Egypt for help." The pattern repeats.
Ezekiel condemns Zedekiah's Egyptian alliance as covenant betrayal -- breaking his oath to Nebuchadnezzar and to God.

notes.note: Zedekiah asks: "Pray to the LORD our God for us." The request implies Zedekiah still hopes for a miraculous deliverance. The answer will disappoint.
The Babylonians withdraw because of Egypt -- but will return. The temporary relief is a test of faith: will Judah assume God has intervened or listen to the prophet?
The hyperbole makes the point: even wounded soldiers could burn Jerusalem. The city's destruction is not contingent on Babylonian military strength but on divine decree.

Calvin warns against self-deception based on temporary relief: "God sometimes removes a trial briefly to test whether the heart has truly changed or merely waited for the pressure to ease.". Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Pharaoh Hophra (Greek: Apries, 589-570 BC) was the fourth king of the 26th Dynasty. His military intervention was brief and ineffective -- he retreated without engaging Babylon directly. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom notes that the Egyptian episode is "the final political miscalculation." Every time Judah relies on Egypt rather than God, the result is worse than before. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann reads the hyperbole as "theological assertion disguised as military analysis." The issue is not whether Babylon can be defeated but whether God has decreed the city's destruction. Military calculation is irrelevant to divine decree. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: The Egyptian army under Pharaoh Hophra (Apries) marches north, causing the Babylonians to lift the siege temporarily (v.5). Hope surges in Jerusalem. Zedekiah secretly asks Jeremiah for a word from God. The answer shatters the optimism: the Egyptians will retreat, and the Babylonians will return. "Even if you were to defeat the entire Babylonian army ... the wounded men left in their tents would come out and burn this city" (v.10).

heb.paragraph: Jeremiah is thrown into a vaulted cell (bor) in the house of Jonathan the secretary (v.16). The bor -- a dry cistern converted to a prison cell -- is a recurring image of unjust suffering in the Hebrew Bible (cf. Joseph in Gen 37:24, the psalmist in Ps 40:2). The prophet who proclaimed the word of God now sits in a hole in the ground.

refs.note: Joseph thrown into a cistern by his brothers -- innocent suffering at the hands of kinsmen.
Paul and Silas imprisoned for preaching -- the NT parallel of prophetic imprisonment.

notes.note: Jeremiah is accused of deserting to the enemy. The charge has surface plausibility: he has been telling people to surrender to Babylon. But his intention is property business, not defection.
Zedekiah secretly asks for a word. The king is politically unable to support Jeremiah publicly but spiritually unable to ignore him. The private consultation reveals Zedekiah's tragic weakness.

Jeremiah negotiates: "Do not send me back to Jonathan's house, or I will die there." Zedekiah orders transfer to the courtyard of the guard with a daily bread ration. Even in crisis, small mercies matter.

Calvin observes Zedekiah's pathetic position: "He wants truth but fears the consequences of acting on it. The king who secretly seeks the prophet while publicly permitting his persecution is the picture of moral cowardice.". Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The captain of the guard, Irijah son of Shelemiah, is from the family of Hananiah -- possibly the same Hananiah whose false prophecy Jeremiah contradicted in chapter 28. Family grudges may fuel the arrest. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom identifies a pattern: each private consultation between Zedekiah and Jeremiah (37:17, 38:14) produces the same answer. The king keeps hoping for a different word; the prophet keeps delivering the same one. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann calls Zedekiah "the most tragic figure in Jeremiah -- a man who recognizes truth but cannot act on it because he fears his own officials more than he fears God.". This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: When the siege lifts temporarily, Jeremiah tries to leave Jerusalem to attend to property in Benjamin (possibly related to the field purchase of ch. 32). He is arrested at the Benjamin Gate, accused of defecting to the Babylonians (v.13), beaten, and thrown into a dungeon. Zedekiah secretly retrieves him and asks again: "Is there a word from the LORD?" The answer is unchanged: "You will be delivered into the hands of the king of Babylon" (v.17). Jeremiah negotiates better conditions and is transferred to the courtyard of the guard.

lit.note: Egyptian intervention creates false hope; Jeremiah's arrest creates the narrative setup for the cistern episode of ch. 38.

themes.note: Judgment and Sovereignty both at 8 -- the decree stands regardless of military developments. Prophecy at 8: the unchanged word despite persecution.

hebtext.note: נָשָׁא (nasha') = to lift up, carry, bear

Jeremiah is thrown into a vaulted cell (bor) in the house of Jonathan the secretary (v.16). The bor -- a dry cistern converted to a prison cell -- is a recurring image of unjust suffering in the Hebrew Bible (cf. Joseph in Gen 37:24, the psalmist in Ps 40:2). The prophet who proclaimed the word of God now sits in a hole in the ground.

translations.text: Then the Babylonians will return and attack this city; they will capture it and burn it down.’
And the Chaldeans shall come back and fight against this city. They shall capture it and burn it with fire.
Zedekiah son of Josiah was made king of Judah by Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon; he reigned in place of Jehoiachin son of Jehoiakim.
Zedekiah the son of Josiah, whom Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon made king in the land of Judah, reigned instead of Coniah the son of Jehoiakim.

thread.text: Isaiah warned against Egyptian alliances a century earlier: "Woe to those who go down to Egypt for help." The pattern repeats.
Ezekiel condemns Zedekiah's Egyptian alliance as covenant betrayal -- breaking his oath to Nebuchadnezzar and to God.
Joseph thrown into a cistern by his brothers -- innocent suffering at the hands of kinsmen.
Paul and Silas imprisoned for preaching -- the NT parallel of prophetic imprisonment.

coaching.tip: Egypt's army approaches, and the Babylonian siege lifts temporarily. Jerusalem celebrates. Jeremiah warns: 'Do not deceive yourselves, thinking the Babylonians will leave' (v. 9). The temporary relief is mistaken for permanent deliverance. Jeremiah distinguishes between reprieve and rescue — a distinction the city refuses to make because the latter requires repentance and the former doesn't.

Jeremiah is arrested on suspicion of desertion to the Babylonians (v. 13) — his counsel to surrender has been reinterpreted as treason. The prophet who speaks God's word becomes politically dangerous. Truth-telling in a crisis looks like betrayal to those committed to illusion. Jeremiah's imprisonment is the cost of accuracy in a culture addicted to false hope.

prayer_prompt: As Jeremiah is arrested on a false charge and thrown into a dungeon for telling the truth, consider asking God whether you are willing to speak faithfully even when the audience punishes the messenger.
